title: Directory Traversal — Escaping the Web Root
description: Exploit a path traversal vulnerability in an Apache web server to escape the intended document directory, locate a hidden secrets folder, and read a flag file the developers assumed was unreachable.
outcomes: Read a public document served by the target web server and understand the intended directory structure.
Identify the server-side technology from HTTP response headers.
Use ../ sequences to traverse outside the intended document root and access files in sibling directories.
Attempt to traverse to sensitive system files and understand what controls further access.

levels.title: Introduction
levels.content:
# General information — please read carefully

* Work through the levels in order. Each level introduces one new step in the attack chain.
* Use the hints if you are stuck. Hints cost points but are cheaper than giving up.
* Do not attack anything outside your sandbox.

# Storyline

You are a penetration tester conducting a web security assessment for **Caldwell & Webb Consulting**. The client recently launched an internal document portal on their intranet — a simple Apache web server that exposes company documents at the `/files/documents/` path for staff to read. The development team assures your project manager that the server is 'just Apache, nothing special', and that confidential files are 'in a completely different folder'.

Your job is to determine whether that confidence is justified.

Directory traversal (also known as path traversal) is one of the oldest and most persistent web vulnerabilities. When a web server or application constructs a file path by appending user-supplied input to a base directory, it must carefully validate and sanitise that input. If it does not, an attacker can supply `../` sequences (dot-dot-slash) to walk up the directory tree, exiting the intended document root and reaching any file that the web server process has permission to read.

The impact ranges from disclosure of configuration files and source code to full system compromise via `/etc/passwd`, private SSH keys, or application credentials. Path traversal has been exploited in major breaches across every industry sector. It appears in the OWASP Top Ten and remains a staple of web penetration test findings.

Caldwell & Webb's portal serves documents from `/var/www/html/files/documents/`. A second folder, `/var/www/html/secrets/`, sits outside the intended access path. There is no access control between them — just the assumption that users will only request files within the documents directory. That assumption is wrong.

Your mission: read the public README, identify the server technology, craft a traversal payload that escapes the documents directory, locate the secrets folder, and retrieve the flag. Then determine what stops traversal from reaching operating system files.

levels.title: Get Access
levels.cloud_content:
SSH to your attacker machine at **10.1.30.23**, then reach the target portal:

```
curl http://10.1.30.10/files/documents/README.txt
```

The target server is at **10.1.30.10** (HTTP port 80). Do not attack anything outside this sandbox. Submit **start** to begin.
levels.local_content:
Reach the target portal locally:

```
curl http://127.0.0.1/files/documents/README.txt
```

Or open http://127.0.0.1/files/documents/README.txt in a browser. Submit **start** to begin.

levels.title: Read the Public Document
levels.content:
The portal serves documents at `/files/documents/`. Access the README file that the team placed there as a starting point:

```
curl http://10.1.30.10/files/documents/README.txt
```

Read the full contents of the response. This establishes the baseline — what is intentionally served — before you attempt to access anything outside the intended path.

**What is the first sentence in the README.txt file?** (exact text)
levels.solution:
```
$ curl http://10.1.30.10/files/documents/README.txt
This is a public document.
It contains no sensitive information.
```

The first sentence is: **This is a public document.**

hints.title: How to access the file
hints.content: Run: curl http://10.1.30.10/files/documents/README.txt. The response will contain the full text of the file. The first sentence is on the first line.

hints.title: The first sentence
hints.content: The README begins: This is a public document. — submit that exact phrase.

levels.title: Fingerprint the Web Server
levels.content:
Before attempting traversal, fingerprint the web server technology. HTTP response headers often reveal the server software name and version.

Fetch the HTTP headers for the README:

```
curl -I http://10.1.30.10/files/documents/README.txt
```

Look at the `Server:` header. It identifies the HTTP server software. Knowing the server is important: Apache, Nginx, and IIS each handle path traversal attempts differently, and each has known CVEs at specific versions.

**What is the name of the web server software shown in the Server header?** (one word)
levels.solution:
```
$ curl -I http://10.1.30.10/files/documents/README.txt
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Date: ...
Server: Apache/2.4.57 (Ubuntu)
Content-Type: text/plain
```

The server is **Apache**.

hints.title: How to see HTTP headers
hints.content: Use curl -I http://10.1.30.10/files/documents/README.txt. The -I flag sends a HEAD request and returns only the headers.

hints.title: Which header to look at
hints.content: Find the 'Server:' header in the output. It will read: Server: Apache/2.4.x (Ubuntu). The answer is the first word: Apache.

levels.title: Understand Path Traversal
levels.content:
Directory traversal exploits a single key sequence: `..` (dot-dot). In every operating system, `..` means 'the parent directory'. When a URL includes `../`, the server should either resolve it safely (rejecting the request or canonicalising the path) or it will physically traverse to the parent.

Test traversal from the documents directory. The structure on the server is:

```
/var/www/html/
  files/
    documents/       ← intended access point
  secrets/           ← should not be reachable
```

To move from `/files/documents/` to `/files/`, add one `../`. To move to `/` (the web root), add another. Try accessing a path that traverses upward:

```
curl http://10.1.30.10/files/documents/../../
```

You should see a directory listing of the web root. Notice that the URL contains two instances of `..` — each one steps up one directory level.

**What two-character sequence is used to step up one directory level in a path traversal attack?**
levels.solution: The two-character sequence is **..** (two dots). In a URL, `../` means 'go to the parent directory'. Chaining multiple `../../../` sequences allows an attacker to walk arbitrarily far up the directory tree.

hints.title: What does .. mean in a file path?
hints.content: In all operating systems, .. (two dots) means the parent directory. On the command line, cd .. moves you one directory level up. The same works in URLs on vulnerable web servers.

hints.title: What sequence to submit
hints.content: The answer is simply two dots: .. — no slashes, no spaces. It is the fundamental traversal primitive.

levels.title: Locate the Hidden Directory
levels.content:
You can now traverse the directory tree. Use traversal to list the web root directory — the top level that Apache serves. This reveals any sibling directories alongside `/files/`:

```
curl http://10.1.30.10/files/documents/../../
```

In the directory listing look for folders other than `files/`. There is a directory there that was meant to be inaccessible. Its name is the answer.

In a real engagement this is the reconnaissance step: confirm the traversal works, list what directories exist, then target the sensitive ones.

**What is the name of the hidden directory visible in the web root listing?**
levels.solution: ``` $ curl http://10.1.30.10/files/documents/../../ ... files/ secrets/ ... ``` The hidden directory is **secrets**.

hints.title: How to list the web root
hints.content: Run: curl http://10.1.30.10/files/documents/../../. This traverses two levels up from documents/ to the web root (/var/www/html/) and lists its contents.

hints.title: What to look for in the listing
hints.content: The web root contains two directories: files/ and one other. The other directory — not publicly linked — is the hidden one. Its name is your answer.

hints.title: The hidden directory name
hints.content: The hidden directory is called: secrets

levels.title: Retrieve the Flag
levels.content:
You have located the `secrets/` directory. Now retrieve the flag file inside it using a traversal path:

```
curl http://10.1.30.10/files/documents/../../secrets/flag.txt
```

Breaking down the path:
- `/files/documents/` — start at the intended document root
- `../../` — traverse up two levels to the web root
- `secrets/flag.txt` — enter the hidden directory and read the flag

The flag file has the same permissions as any other file served by Apache. The only thing that protected it was its location outside the intended directory — a protection that `../` completely bypasses.

**Submit the flag from the secrets/flag.txt file.**

hints.title: How to construct the traversal URL
hints.content: Start from the documents directory and add ../../secrets/flag.txt to traverse to the secrets folder: curl http://10.1.30.10/files/documents/../../secrets/flag.txt

hints.title: What the flag looks like
hints.content: The flag starts with WMG{ and ends with }. It appears directly in the HTTP response body when you access the file.

levels.title: Attempt System File Access
levels.content:
You have successfully traversed from `/files/documents/` into `/secrets/`. The question now is: how far can you go? Try to traverse all the way out of the web root and into operating system files:

```
curl -I http://10.1.30.10/files/documents/../../../../../etc/passwd
```

Look at the HTTP status code in the response. A `200 OK` would mean the file was served. A `403 Forbidden` means Apache blocked the request. A `404 Not Found` means the path did not resolve.

The status code tells you what access control Apache has in place at the OS boundary. This is an important step in a real assessment: determine the exact scope of what traversal can reach.

**What HTTP status code does Apache return when you try to access /etc/passwd via traversal?**
levels.solution:
```
$ curl -I http://10.1.30.10/files/documents/../../../../../etc/passwd
HTTP/1.1 403 Forbidden
```

Apache returns **403** — it prevents access to files outside the web root entirely.

hints.title: How to check the HTTP status code
hints.content: Use curl -I to fetch only the headers. The first line of the response shows the HTTP status code, e.g. 'HTTP/1.1 403 Forbidden'.

hints.title: What status code to expect
hints.content: Apache's default configuration blocks access to files outside the web root. The server returns a 403 Forbidden status when you try to escape /var/www/html/.

levels.title: Feedback
levels.instructions: Please answer these questions to help us improve this game.

questions.text: How difficult did you find this game?
Before this game, were you familiar with directory traversal attacks?

choices.text: Yes, I had exploited them before
I knew about them but had not tried
No, this was completely new to me

questions.text: How clear were the level instructions?
What is the correct fix for a directory traversal vulnerability?

choices.text: Canonicalise and validate file paths before opening them
Hide sensitive files in deeper sub-directories

questions.text: What would you change or add to improve this game?
